title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is concentrated in evaluation and robustness rather than policy: several fresh papers introduce benchmarks or methods that probe whether AI systems behave safely, follow instructions, and rely on the right evidence. The strongest updates focus on safety testing for embodied models, subgroup robustness, enterprise-agent evaluation, and checks against models substituting memorized priors for grounded inputs.

items.title: LIBERO-Safety benchmarks physical and semantic safety in vision-language-action models
items.why_it_matters: It adds a dedicated safety benchmark for embodied models operating under hazardous physical and instruction constraints.

items.title: Discovering Latent Groups for Robust Classification
items.why_it_matters: It targets hidden subgroup failures that standard accuracy masks, a core issue in fairness and reliability.

items.title: EnterpriseClawBench uses real workplace sessions to benchmark enterprise agents
items.why_it_matters: It could improve evaluation of agents handling sensitive files, tools, and business workflows in realistic settings.

items.title: The Table Says Otherwise tests whether LLMs read tables or recall memorized facts
items.why_it_matters: It probes grounding failures where models ignore provided evidence and answer from pretraining instead.

items.title: Flatness Preserves Instruction Following in Vision-Language-Action Models
items.why_it_matters: It studies how finetuning can erode instruction following and make embodied policies rely on unsafe shortcuts.

items.title: DiT-Reward explores generative representations for text-to-image reward modeling
items.why_it_matters: Better reward models can strengthen evaluation and alignment of image generators beyond surface-level preference signals.

items.title: Why Machines Misread Pedagogical Quality examines human-machine alignment in LLM evaluation
items.why_it_matters: It highlights evaluator misalignment, a broader warning for using LLMs to judge nuanced human-quality criteria.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
